Within minutes, the device began registering microscopic changes in pressure, like a pulse underwater, slow and massive. When they hauled the boy back up, the underside was slick with a clear jelly, not biological, not synthetic. and when touched it evaporated instantly into scentless vapor. The footage was reviewed. The drone's recordings were verified. Meteorologists dismissed it as an atmospheric illusion, but the visuals remain a fully formed cloud drifting down like a feather into the ocean and vanishing into depth without struggle. No one knows what pulled it down. Some believe it wasn't a cloud at all, but something mimicking one. Something that rose once and returned to where it came from. A breath exhaled by the sea, or an eye closing and somewhere deep below the waves. That softness still falls in the dead of polar night beneath the solid white sheet of the wed sea, a glacial shelf cracked just wide enough to let a submersible pass. The mission was routine. Scan for microbial life. Chart brine channels. Listen for creeks in the old ice. But as the drone sank deeper into the cavity, a low sound began to emerge. A breath, not wind, not current. A slow, resonant inhale that pressed against the hull like warmth without heat. Then came the exhale. Deep, steady, stretched across 10 full seconds. Ice didn't cause it. Pressure didn't explain it. The sound repeated every 12 minutes. Microphones recorded the pulse across a 40 km radius, echoing beneath the ice, like lungs embedded in the continent. Seismic data ruled out fault shifts. Marine biologists found no signs of large mammals. And yet, the rhythm continued, so steady, so deliberate, it felt like a ritual. Below the shelf, the ice water turned strangely clear. The drone shook slightly. Lights dimmed. instruments held, but the silence afterward was louder than the sound. Crew aboard the research vessel described feeling watched, though nothing had appeared. No motion, no shadow. One technician later discovered a pressure discrepancy in the logs. The drone had dropped half a meter during the final breath, though no ballast had shifted. Near the edge of the Norwegian Sea, in waters normally torn by wind and scattered drift ice, a weather satellite captured a swirl in the surface. A spiral motion too perfect to be storm born, too fixed to belong to current. From orbit it looked like a whirlpool, circular, clean, widening by the hour. But over the next four days, the shape never drifted. It remained locked in position, unmoved by tides, unshaken by storms. Ships that approached the coordinates were met with calm. Not the lull before the Tempest calm, but a dead stillness that muted sound and dulled sensation. Compass needles twitched. Radio frequencies hissed gently, even when silent. The whirlpool wasn't spinning. The water rotated, yes, but the center held like the axis of a turning world. Vessels that circled it reported unease. Instruments didn't fail, but they lagged. Coordinates became delayed by seconds. One ship's chronometer lost 7 minutes without explanation. As though time itself was being stretched across the spiral. A drone was flown overhead. The footage showed a mirror. The spiral moved, but the water in the center reflected perfectly still sky. Oceanographers labeled it a standing eddy, a perfect fluke of wind, water, and time, but the data says otherwise. The whirlpool did not move. The world around it did, as if something beneath the water had paused the surface just long enough to look up. On a routine reef study just off the coast of Papua Newu Guinea, a diver brushing through a bloom of stagghorn coral struck metal. Not hard, not loud, just a delicate tap, the kind that doesn't echo, but rings. Curious, she swept the coral back. Beneath the branching spines, almost fully encased in living limestone, a circle glinted, brass, or something like it, dulled by decades of salt. At first she thought it was a compass, but as she cleared more silt, the details emerged. Not directional markers, but hands. A clock, perfectly round, sealed behind crystal that hadn't cracked. It sat embedded in the reef like a time piece, waiting to be remembered. The coral had grown through its frame, but hadn't broken it. Instead, the branches seemed to curve with it, wrapping gently around the rim like fingers holding something sacred. It was ticking, not fast, not loud, but real, measured, steady. The diver, thinking it a hallucination caused by pressure, recorded it on video. Hours later, back aboard the research vessel, the sound remained. A tick every 1.47 seconds, slower than any mechanical movement should allow. No winding mechanism visible, no battery casing, no source, and yet it kept time sort of. The hands moved in sequence, but not by hour or minute. They followed the tide, shifting forward at high water, retreating at Eb in perfect sync with the lunar pull. The crystal wasn't glass, and the markings were symbols, not numbers. Etched curves that resembled ancient Polynesian star maps. They left the clock in the coral. No one dared remove it. The reef somehow was still growing around it, not consuming, but preserving. A cradle of stone and salt, holding time, not as a measure, but as a memory. Each tick echoes in footage. Each second feels older than the one before. And somewhere beneath the waves, that buried clock still counts, not toward midnight, but towards something else. Something coming back. Near the mid-atlantic ridge, where the seabed splits like a scar and black smokers hiss in eternal exhale, a deep trollwler cast its nets into silence and drew up something strange. The hall was unremarkable at first. Shrimp, rockfish, silt heavy bones until one of the deck hands reached for a glimmer lodged in the mesh. A fish roughly the length of a forearm, unmoving but not stiff. Its body glistened like brushed chrome, smooth and cold, dappled with pressure dents that didn't belong to flesh. It wasn't armor. It was skin, scaled metal, thin as foil, and warm to the touch. No one knew how it bled. A small incision at the base of the tail revealed no tissue, no muscle. Instead, a silvery blue liquid dripped out in rhythm, not like blood, like coolant, as if this thing had been alive once, but only partly so. The eyes were the most human part, rounded, soft, and deeply set, not cameras, not glass, biological, staring with the blank calm of something that had seen pressure no human vessel could survive. They froze it, tagged it, logged the coordinates. But 12 hours later, when the team returned to inspect the body, it was gone. The freezer remained sealed. No power failure, no breach, just one long smear of moisture across the steel floor, ending in a trail of glinting droplets that vanished near the aft deck. Reviewing footage showed nothing. The cameras glitched at exactly 2:11 a.m. 33 seconds of static, then an empty table. Later analysis of the trail found particles not found in Earth's oceanic sediment, micro fragments of alloy, structured, not fused, built. One marine biologist, after running a DNA scan on the only surviving tissue scrap, quietly closed her laptop, and refused to speak for the rest of the voyage. It had let itself be found. Aboard the research vessel, Mistl, anchored above the depths of the South Sandwich trench. A crew member cleaning a sensor dome caught his own reflection in the outer hull. Or so he thought. The surface had been polished to mirror finish by weeks of salt spray and wind, a trick of light meant to be ignored. But when he leaned in, cloth in hand, the face staring back didn't blink. When he did, he froze, watched again, lifted a hand. The reflection lifted the opposite. At first, it seemed like a delay, a glitch in angle, but then the figure smiled. He hadn't. He stepped back and the reflection stepped closer. It lasted only 3 seconds. Then, as the surface trembled with a soft lurch from the tide, the mirror shattered into normaly. The incident was dismissed, a trick of exhaustion, perhaps, the mind playing games under pressure and monotony. In some cases, the reflection turned toward them when they weren't facing it at all. The ship's mirrored dome camera recorded two seconds of static during one of these moments. In Pulburn places, into one of these moments. In the glitch, a row of faces stared directly into the lens. 12 of them, but only eight people were on board. Don't look if it looks first. Off the edge of the sangi ark, where the seabed falls into shadows too deep for maps and the water pulses warm from unseen vents, a solo descent was scheduled for routine data collection. The diver, a seasoned veteran of trench work and long exposures, entered the capsule without hesitation. Cameras rolled, tethers secured. Descent began at 9:02. The dive was expected to last 40 minutes. At 31 minutes, contact was lost, not severed, blurred. The signal didn't break. It just slowed like time on the other end had begun to stretch. One moment the voice was clear, the next it lagged behind its own words, syllables stretching into static, as though echoing from further away than the depth allowed. Visual feed fuzzed to grayscale, then black, and then at 10:17, the capsule breached the surface again, alone. The diver was seated inside, breathing, awake, but changed. Hair now gray where it had been black, skin weathered, creased, eyes sunken with a look not of fear, but of distance, like someone who had spent decades looking inward. His first words, "How long was I gone?" They told him, "Just over an hour." He wept. Medics ruled out chemical exposure. No radiation, no neurological trauma, but his blood work returned strange inconsistencies. Cellular aging markers far beyond his previous baseline. Scar tissue in places he'd never been injured. Calcium buildup in his bones. He hadn't hallucinated. He had aged physically irrefutably. Asked what he'd seen, he said nothing for days. Then late one night, he requested a pen and wrote a single page, a diagram. Impossible to decipher. Spirals within spirals, time codes, coordinates, and one phrase repeated over and over down the margin. It watches forward. It remembers backward. He never dove again. No one has attempted that trench since, but to this day, his old capsule sits in a lab. Its walls lightly scorched from the inside, as if brushed by time itself. And if you sit quietly in the dark beside it, some say you can still hear him breathing from somewhere deeper than the ocean. Beneath the calm shallows of the Seyell's plateau, where sunlight dapples the ocean floor like stained glass across cathedral stone, a team of marine ecologists notice strange lines in the sand. Not natural, not random. Sweeps and angles repeated, deliberate, traced in green. The pattern wasn't carved. It was grown. The outlines of something vast, too symmetrical for nature, too complex for chance. A city rendered in soft chlorophyll and silence. The structures weren't raised. Unlike any known marine life, this new sound did not rise or fall. It did not waver with emotion. It pulsed evenly, consistently every 29 seconds. Slow spirals too structured to be convection, too fluid to be mechanical. Every time it pulsed, nearby instruments lagged by a fraction of a second. GPS drifted. Lights dimmed. A soft vibration tickled fingertips, even through gloves, as if it were speaking in silence. The ship's medical officer, Curious, synchronized the pulse to a human heart monitor. Beneath the cold expanse of the Labrador Sea, where icy waters churn and currents weave slow patterns across the continental shelf, a strange flow has long puzzled oceanographers. Known to locals as the black current, it moves against the dominant tide, warm, dark, and silent, carrying with it an energy that defies explanation. Unlike the icy layers surrounding it, the black current slips through like a shadow, thick with warmth, heavy but fluid, carrying a heat signature that tests the limits of known geothermal sources. Instruments lowered into its path, register a temperature spike, subtle yet persistent, rising as the current travels northward beneath ice shelves that should have frozen solid. No wind stirs its rise, no storms herald its approach. It leaves no wake, no churning foam, only an eerie calm broken by the soft lapping of water against hulls. Scientific expeditions sought to understand the phantom tide. Satellite imagery revealed fleeting changes in sea surface height, but these shifts were subtle, vanishing before data could be fully gathered. Sonar scans picked up deep underwater currents that seemed to defy the expected flow patterns, twisting and turning through submarine canyons and fishes with uncanny precision. Divers who ventured close spoke of disorientation and vertigo. The water seemed to pulse with an energy that blurred the boundary between time and space. Instruments malfunctioned, compasses spun unpredictably, and radio signals crackled with static. Those caught in the swell reported a sense of being carried not just through water, but through moments, memories folding and unfolding like waves upon the tide. Local legends paint the phantom tide as the ocean's breath, a living pulse that reminds the world of its ancient untamed spirit. Some say it is a gateway, a passage between realms where the sea's surface and the depths below blur into one. Others whisper of guardians beneath the waves, entities that command the tide as a tool of protection or warning. The phantom tide challenges understanding. It is both presence and absence, a force seen and unseen, known yet unknowable. It reminds those who witness it that the ocean is a realm of mysteries far deeper than the eye can perceive. And as long as the phantom tide rises and falls, the sea keeps its secrets, waiting for those who dare to listen to the ghostly rhythm beneath the waves. The sea exhales, a gentle sigh that ripples across the vastness of water. Soothing and soft, the tides retreat into silence, carrying with them the echoes of mysteries whispered in the deep. secrets the ocean holds close, shared only in the quietest moments. Beneath the surface, the ocean breathes slowly. Currents glide like soft fingers across the darkened reefs, brushing past coral and creatures unseen. The water moves with a rhythm older than time itself. A lullaby written in waves and whispered in currents. Close your eyes and listen. Feel the gentle pulse beneath the surface. The heartbeat of the ocean soul, steady and slow. The pulse that has sung to the stars that has cradled ancient creatures and borne the weight of forgotten worlds. Imagine the deep, a place where light fades into blue and black, where silence wraps you like a velvet cloak. The ocean floor stretches wide and mysterious, alive with shadows that dance just beyond your vision. Here the secrets rest, not in noise or chaos, but in stillness and grace. The waves lapse softly against the shore, a rhythm like the slow breathing of the earth. Each crest rises and falls, a gentle reminder that all things flow, that all things return. The ocean's voice is not harsh or loud. It is a whisper, a song, a quiet hymn to the endless cycle of life. Let the sound carry you. Drift with the currents as they move through ancient trenches and coral gardens, past glowing creatures that pulse with their own light. Feel the cool embrace of water, the gentle pressure that holds you safe and warm beneath the surface. Beneath the waves, time slows. The world above fades. Thoughts settle like sediment, floating down to rest in the soft sand. Your mind quiets, body lightens, and breath deepens. In the distance, the ocean calls, a melody without words, without form, but full of meaning. It sings of journeys taken, of mysteries solved and unsolved, of the endless dance between sea and sky. The ocean is ancient, vast, and alive. It holds your dreams as it holds its own. Cradled in the deep blue. Let yourself drift. Sink deeper. Float free. The sea awaits a timeless lullabi. And in its embrace find peace.

title: Strange Things Found In the Deep Sea
transcript:
- [Narrator] Our oceans cover more than 70% of the Earth's surface, but did you know that over
80% of them remain unexplored? Being too deep, too
cold, or too dangerous, these places are home
to secrets most people have never seen, until now. From really freaky fish
to inexplicable objects, get your flippers and oxygen tank ready as we uncover the
strangest things ever found in the deep sea. (light suspenseful music) - Whoa. (bell dings) - [Narrator] The eldritch horror. Deep down in the ocean, you expect to see some
pretty crazy creatures. The pressure of all the water, and lack of sunlight
from all the refraction, means that what lives down
here has had to adapt. This can mean bigger eyes to pick up as much light as possible, or spongier bodies to deal
with the pressure, or... What? What the heck is that? (sparse eerie music) I can't tell where its tentacles
end, and its head begins. If it even has a head. What is that thing? The footage of this roiling nightmare was captured by the SERPENT
project back in 2015, off the coast of Angola in
the South Atlantic Ocean. Using a remote operated vehicle, they dived to 4,350 feet below the waves when they stumbled upon
this bizarre creature. Initially, they had no
idea what it could be, aside from the wriggling,
writhing spawn of Cthulhu. But, after analyzing its features, they concluded that it's not,
in fact, an eldritch horror, but a kind of siphonophore, a colony of marine organisms
that work together as a hive, though, to us humans,
they simply appear to be one incredibly creepy entity. However, this hive-like structure means there's no uniform shape or size that a colony takes on, as the EV Nautilus team
revealed back in 2014. At some 200 feet down in the waters off the
southern coast of the USA, they came across this
otherworldly siphonophore. It looks completely different from the sentient knot we saw before, but these two organism colonies
belong to the same family. Ah, because the horror of looking on one wasn't enough I take it. Doll discovery. Thanks to its imperious 880-foot-long size and status as the "unsinkable" ship, the Titanic has literally
gone down in history as the world's most notorious shipwreck. Yet, despite that, concrete
evidence of the Titanic's fate wasn't discovered until 1985, 74 years after it hit an iceberg and sank. But fisherman Abel Federico Nogueiras actually uncovered the Titanic's fate eight years before the
whole shipwreck was found. Back in 1976, in the middle
of the Atlantic Ocean, he hauled up the net of his trawler and spotted something that
didn't look like a fish. As he leaned in to get a better look, he was shocked to see an eyeless, lifeless face staring right back at him. But it wasn't a human, it
was a porcelain doll head. Fished from some 6,500 feet below, this wasn't any old kid's toy. As it happened, Nogueiras had been fishing in the same area of the Atlantic Ocean that the Titanic sunk. After Nogueiras passed away in 1992, his son sold the porcelain
piece to Teresa Martin, a doll collector. She delved deeper into its history, uncovering documents about
girls on board the ship that had brought dolls with them. She discovered that
Titanic survivor Eva Hart had written in her memoirs
that she left her doll in her cabin when she evacuated the ship. It then turned out that
the make of the doll head was Shoenau & Hoffmeister 1906, the very same manufacturer of Hart's doll. Whoa. Okay, whatever you do, no one
tell James Cameron about this. Ancient artifact. If you think 100-year-old
Titanic artifacts are ancient, they're nothing compared to
another deep-sea discovery made in 1984. Marine archeologist Ehud Galili was diving off the
coast of Atlit in Israel in the hope of finding a shipwreck. While he didn't stumble
across a broken boat, he did discover something
else that piqued his interest. 40 feet under the waves, Galili spotted the remnants
of rectangular houses. He reported the finding, leading to more underwater
excavations of the area. Soon graves, human remains, these water wells that look
like portals into another world, and even seven megaliths
arranged in a stone semicircle emerged from this marine metropolis. If that wasn't crazy enough, scientists carbon-dated the site, finding it to be around 8,500 years old. For reference, that makes
this place 4,000 years older than the Pyramids of Giza. And as for the massive megalith, it's around 3,500 years
older than Stonehenge. The question is, why were the remains of this
once-special city submerged? Was it part of some ancient aqua empire? Well, scientists believe
that the settlement, which is about eight times the size of a standard football field,
was suddenly abandoned. Why? A study estimated that at the time that Atlit Yam was present, an eruption at Mount
Etna in Southern Italy caused a 130-foot-high tsunami to engulf Mediterranean coastal cities within a matter of hours. If only they'd constructed
some flood defenses instead of those massive megaliths. Strange stalkers. The deep sea isn't just home
to artifacts from a bygone era, it's also the place for
some curious creatures that are very much alive
in the present day. Take this grizzly guy, better
known as a deepsea lizardfish. Man, I'm gonna struggle
getting to sleep tonight. But, scary as these fish look, I wouldn't worry too much
about bumping into one. That's because these
two-foot-long critters only live at depths around 2,000 feet to 11,000 feet. This part of the ocean is
known as the midnight zone. It's a place of almost constant darkness, except for light from
any bioluminescent beasts swimming around. The lack of light means
that deepsea lizardfish have to adapt. To detect prey, they
use their enormous eyes to amplify what little light reaches down to those deep depths. Yet eerie eyes aren't the
only frightening feature of this creature. Their monster mouths are
filled with hundreds of sharp, needle-like teeth, used for
piercing and trapping prey. Deepsea lizardfish are one of the world's deepest-living apex predators, and won't hesitate to
eat anything they find down in the dark,
including their own kind. Though if they do run
across another lizardfish, there's an equal chance they'll
get randy with it instead. Huh? Okay, not where I thought this was going. Because of how dark and
sparse of life it is at the depths these fish thrive at, all deepsea lizardfish are hermaphroditic, meaning they have male and
female reproductive organs, so they can breed with any
other member of their species. Well, that puts a whole
new spin on the phrase "playing with your food." Standing strong. Traveling even further down to the deeper, darker
depths of 15,000 feet, we find another very odd organism. The tripod fish, as it's aptly called, is found in the abyssal zone, a section right below the midnight zone that stretches from 13,000 to 20,000 feet. And it's here where the
tripod fish does its thing. Its thing being standing up with its three elongated
projections from its fins. Unlike deepsea lizardfish, these critters aren't
blessed with enormous eyes. Instead, they possess peepers that are barely a millimeter in diameter. You'd think that'd make
hunting tricky for tripod fish, but not so. Once settled on the deep seabed, this freaky fish turns its
head into the direction of water movement. Then, it waits motionless until it detects any potential meals, except not with its eyes
but extended pectoral fins. These hover above its
head like an antenna, picking up swimming vibrations
from any approaching prey. Thanks to the desolate
depths tripod fish inhabit, they, like the deepsea lizardfish,
share a lonely existence, so are also hermaphroditic. Except, not only are these funky fish able to mate with any other
member of their species, they can even make both sperm and eggs to produce offspring all by themselves. Talk about being resourceful. Open wide. Back up at 10,000 feet, and
re-entering the midnight zone, is another creepy creature that has to be seen to be believed. Known as the gulper eel or pelican eel, this deep sea find is most
notable for its massive maw, which, surprise, resembles
that of a pelican. In total, these guys measure
around 2 1/2 feet long, with their head taking up around 25% of their overall length. But their chops aren't only big, they're also loosely hinged,
allowing them to open wide, with the loose skin of their underside creating a massive net that can scoop up prey twice their size. But the pelican eel's funky features don't end with their massive mouths. At the other end of their
anatomy is a whip-like tail that doubles up as a light-producing organ known as a photophore. Through bioluminescence, the
photophore glows bright pink, illuminating the dark
waters around the eel. You might think that this blows its cover, (sharp dramatic music) but the light acts as a lure, attracting prey into the
massive mouth of these monsters. Can you think of anything worse than this being your last sight? Well, whatever you do,
don't go towards the light. Colossal cutter. Odd organisms aren't the
only thing in the deep blue that'll leave you scratching your head. What would you do if you found
a giant knife-like object whilst scuba diving? I mean, just take a look
at this perplexing picture. No, don't rub your eyes, those divers are indeed
holding a gigantic knife. This daunting discovery, found by divers off the
coast of Madagascar, has left people on the internet stumped. Presuming that those guys are adult-size, then that knife's gotta
be over seven feet long. The question is, who
needs a knife that big? Some theories suggests
that this whopping weapon was used by some prehistoric giants that walked the Earth tens
of thousands of years ago. Who knows, maybe this blade
was used to strike down some super-sized shark,
like the megalodon? In truth, it's likely
that this ominous object isn't actually a knife at all. Instead, it's far more probable that this is a lost prop from a movie that somehow ended up in the ocean. Personally, I reckon it's
an aircraft propeller that snapped from a fallen plane or a rudder or daggerboard
that broke off a boat. What do you think? Tell you what? Hit subscribe if you think that this find is from a lost movie set, and hit like if you think it's
from an airplane propellor. If you have your own theories, why don't you let me know
down in the comments below? Sea sculptures. At first glance, Molinere
Bay off the coast of Grenada looks as pretty as a postcard. But all is not as it seems under the water's surface
of this beautiful bay. You see, any scuba diver that
dives under the waves here will be confronted by this haunting sight. Yep, that's it, I'm never
going in the sea again. Fortunately, this isn't a dark, twisted, underwater version of Pompeii, these concrete figures are manmade. British sculptor Jason deCaires Taylor constructed the sculptures from life casts of the local community, not to fill divers with dread but to engage people with
the underwater environment that surround them. Man, imagine going on a
pretty, picturesque scuba dive when suddenly you're confronted by that. Around 4,500 miles north of Molinere Bay, there's an even creepier
underwater statue. 134 feet below the
surface of Lake Neuchatel, you'll find this 16-foot-long
spine-chilling shark sculpture that'll ensure you experience
some nautical nightmares. The question is, why's there
a 16-foot-long terror shark inhabiting a lake in Switzerland? Well, this burly beast was made in 2007 and used as a prob for a short
film called "Choc au Lac!" After the movie was finished, the scary shark sculpture
was dumped in the lake, presumably to attract and scare tourists. I mean, can you imagine
swimming in that lake without knowing that marine
monster was at the bottom? I think my wetsuit would
get a little more wet. See ya later, alligator. Speaking of powerful predators
lurking in deep waters, that brings us onto
our next fearsome find, located over 6,000 feet
below the water's surface in the Gulf of Mexico. Yep, your eyes don't deceive you, that is an alligator at the
bottom of the deep blue. Except, unlike that scary shark,
this gator is no sculpture, it's 100% real. Can you imagine how scary
it'd be for a deep-sea diver to come face-to-face with one
of these powerful predators? The question is, how can an alligator, an animal normally found
in rivers and lakes, survive in the deep sea? The answer? They don't. You see, this alligator isn't alive. Turns out three dead gators
were part of an experiment, with marine biologists from the Louisiana
Universities Marine Consortium dropping them down into the deep sea to observe how creatures
that thrive down there react to an unusual food source. The results shocked them, with one whole alligator being devoured by football-sized isopods
in less than 24 hours. Another specimen was gnawed
right down to its bone, leaving a strange brown fuzz behind. DNA studies revealed
that this weird residue was actually a newly discovered
species of bone-eating worm. Like the deep sea
couldn't get any stranger. And as for the third carcass, well, researchers don't actually know. The entire gator went
missing after eight days, with just the deployment harness and weight used to sink it remaining. On further investigation, the scientists discovered drag marks where they'd originally left
the 40-pound heavy gator. To this day, they still don't
know what colossal critter had the power and stomach to
ravage an entire alligator. That's deeply unsettling. Turtle tomb. Now, alligators aren't the only reptiles to end up in the sea's depths. Just ask photographer Josh Vergara. When he went diving off the coast of Sipadan Island in Malaysia, he was expecting to catch
some mesmerizing marine shots. Instead, he uncovered something that sent a shiver down his spine. Around 75 feet underwater,
Vergara stumbled across a cave, but it was no ordinary cave. This cavern was chock
full of deceased turtles. The cave, known by locals
as the "Turtle Tomb," is filled with dozens
of shelled skeletons. Now, sea turtles are
famously solitary creatures, so how come so many skeletons can be found in this graveyard? Well, some believe that the turtles chose this cave as a resting place, seeing out their final
moments here in peace. But sadly, the more probable explanation isn't so Disney-esque. The cave is filled with
plenty of twists and turns, so it's more likely they each
ventured in at some point and become disoriented. While turtles can hold their breath for an impressive two hours, it's likely that, not
knowing how to get out, they all asphyxiated. That's as sad as it is strange. Scary shell. How about we focus on
some deep-sea critters that are alive and kicking! Or swimming, should I say? This cute little crustacean
is a hermit crab. These guys have a soft abdomen, unlike the hard, calcified abdomens in most other crustaceans. So, to protect their
bodies from predators, hermit crabs think resourcefully. Typically, these guys use
empty sea snail shells. But this handy hermit had a bolder idea. Now, it may look like
some sort of alien gunk, but this is actually a
Parapagurus hermit crab, using a sea anemone-like animal known as a zoanthid as a shell. When hermit crabs are small,
they pick up a sea snail shell, then, at some point, the
zoanthid attaches to it. As the hermit crab grows,
so does the zoanthid, beginning to take on the
role of the original shell, coiling around the abdomen
of the hermit crab, leading to this horror. Disturbing as it looks,
it's actually all good. The crab avoids the dangerous
task of swapping shells, which briefly leaves them exposed, while the stinging cells in
the tentacles of the zoanthids deter any predators from
snacking on the hermit. This symbiotic relationship
benefits the zoanthids too as the crab's mobility
allows the zoantharians to access different areas
of the ocean for feeding. Sounds like a match made in heaven, even if it looks like it's made in hell. Beastly blimp. In April 2023, Roman Fedortsov went
fishing in the Barents Sea off the northern coast of Russia. He was working on a commercial trawler, looking to catch the usual: Cod, haddock, maybe mackerel,
too, if he was lucky. However, that's not all
that showed up in his net. With the nets able to reach
down as far as 3,300 feet, it's not uncommon for creepy creatures to be hauled into the ship as well. And that fateful day, Fedortsov hauled up this fearsome fish. Its fleshy pink mouth, scaly
black body, lizard-like tail, and grotesquely protruding eyes make this pure nightmare fuel. After posting a picture of it online, theories about what this
organism was started to sprout. Some wondered if it
was a rare abnormality, others blamed pollution
for these dreadful defects. Turns out, it was neither. This fish is an onion-eyed grenadier, a ray-finned fish that thrives
around 3,000 feet down. Now, these guys have
all the funky features of Fedortsov's find, except
for a couple of things. Firstly, they don't share the
same distended pink mouth. And secondly, while their eyes are large, they don't normally appear to
be popping out of their head. The bulging eyes and mouth
are caused by barotrauma, the physical damage to body tissues caused by a change in pressure. Fish have a swim bladder,
an internal gas-filled organ that helps them maintain buoyancy at different depths in the water. At the deep depths the
onion-eyed grenadier experiences, the pressure is high, meaning gases within the
swim bladder are compressed. But, when a fish is rapidly
brought to the surface, the external pressure
significantly decreases. As a result, the compressed gases in the swim bladder expand, leading to the fish's surrounding organs and tissue popping out. Hmm, think I preferred
the pollution explanation. Nautical nuke. Over time, ocean floors have become home to some mysterious manmade products. Take this image that's
circulated around the web. That is what looks like a nuclear reactor embedded into sediment on the sea floor. Which would be terrifying,
if it wasn't Photoshop. But don't breathe too easy because there are nuclear reactors scattered throughout the
seas and lots of them. Between the 1960s and 1980s,
the Soviet Union dumped around 18,000 radioactive objects
into the Barents Sea alone. Yep, that very same sea
with that freaky fish. Most of the objects pose
little environmental risk. However, some are
increasingly seen as a hazard to the ecosystems in the
sea of Northern Russia. Some of the most radioactive objects are the nuclear reactors
from submarines K-11, K-19, K-27, K-140, and K-159, as well as the nuclear reactor that served the Lenin icebreaker. It's believed that these six objects account for more than 90%
of the radioactive sources dumped in the Barents Sea. The problem is removing nuclear time bombs from depths stretching down over 650 feet is not an easy task, requiring
specialist equipment, experts, and a lot of manpower. This means that the operation to remove just those six objects alone would cost close to $300 million. Yeah, I don't think those reactors are gonna be moving for a while. Marine monster. But it's not just the deep Barents Sea that's full of ghoulish horrors. Traveling 4,000 miles southeast
of those wicked waters, you'll find the Pacific Ocean. It's here that back in 2010, researchers of the Japan
Agency for Marine-Earth Science and Technology, or JAMSTEC, were remotely operating
an underwater vehicle, plunging it down to a depth of 1,000 feet, and recording what they found. Looks pretty standard: Water, seabed, but then
it got less standard and way spookier. (sparse eerie music) What the hell is that? A demonic sea cow? The Pokemon Muk come to life? After seeing this footage, some people have even suggested
this could be the Ningen, a cryptid creature from modern folklore. Researchers reportedly once spotted this humanoid marine beast
off the coast of Antarctica, estimating it to be around
65 to 100 feet long. For reference, that's around
the same size as a blue whale. If that wasn't ominous
enough, the Ningen's also said to have large vacuous eyes
and five-fingered hands. Now, we can't get a good
look at the full size or limbs of this critter, but those eyes do look pretty
soul-sucking, don't they? Maybe the Ningen isn't just folklore. That being said, the researchers
operating the vehicle believe it not to be the Ningen, but just a rock with a few
barnacles attached to it, giving it the illusion of having eyes. What do you think, though? Barnacle-covered rock? Or a super-sized humanoid marine monster that's about to destroy the world? I know what my money's on. The Ningen. Are you squidding me? But for all the strange deep-sea finds we've uncovered so far, there's
one that trumps them all. Why? Well, for starters, it's
found in the Hadal zone, the deepest part of the ocean. Located at the bottom of oceanic trenches, more people have been to the
moon than the Hadal zone. If you did somehow make it
here, I'd keep your eyes peeled, just in case your submarine
bumps into this ocean oddball. Man, it's like if
Slenderman took squid form. But, while Slenderman is a cryptid that only exists on the internet, this thing is absolutely real. Living at head-bursting
depths of around 20,000 feet, this creepy creature goes
by the name of Magnapinna, or bigfin squid. And true to its name, these
guys have some pretty big fins. Well, technically, tentacles. Unlike other squid, their appendages are held
at elbow-like joints, with the main stretch of
the tentacles themselves suspended below the squid's body. In all, these appendages can
stretch some 26 feet long, making them close to
three times the height of an NBA basketball hoop. Despite their stretchy size,
the depth these guys live at mean they've only been
spotted a handful of times, and so much is unknown about them. Like, how do these beasts hunt? One theory is that they
drag their tall tentacles along the seafloor, grabbing
whatever edible organisms cross their perilous path. Another is that they simply float, waiting passively for
prey like zooplankton to get trapped in their tentacles. However, these guys hunt, coming eyeball to eyeball
with a bigfin squid four miles beneath the water's surface might leave you with an
ac-squid-ent in your pants. (suspenseful drum rolling music) After all this time, we'd like
to officially introduce you to myself, Jay, and Wesley. We are the narrators here at Be Amazed, but we also know you guys
tend to have a favorite for certain videos and topics. So, are you Team Wesley or Team Jay? Please click on the poll in
the description to let me know. Phew! That rounds up some of the
more nightmarish things ever found in the deep sea. Which ocean discovery left
you scared of the water? Let me know in the comments
below, and thanks for watching. (gentle thoughtful music)

title: They Found Something Terrifying… That’s Why NASA Stopped Exploring the Ocean
transcript: Why NASA stopped exploring the ocean? The untold truth behind NASA's deep sea mystery. The National Aeronautics and Space Administration, better known as NASA, is famous for its incredible missions to space. From landing humans on the moon to sending rovers to Mars and exploring distant galaxies, NASA's achievements are legendary. But what many people Don and T know is that NASA was in tea always focused solely on space. In fact, before they became known as the space agency, NASA was deeply involved in ocean exploration. And here I guess where the mystery begins because one question continues to puzzle millions of people around the world. Why did NASA stop exploring the ocean? Let's dive into the truth behind this fascinating and somewhat eerie mystery. NASA's forgotten connection with the ocean. Before NASA became the space exploration giant we know today, it had strong roots in Earth and ocean research. During the 1950s and 1960s, NASA worked closely with the US Navy and oceanographic institutions to study the Earth's atmosphere, climate, and ocean systems. NASA's satellites were designed not just to explore outer space, but to observe our planet from above, tracking ocean currents, mapping the seafloor, and understanding how our planet's water systems work. Even today, NASA operates oceanobserving satellites like Aqua, Terra, and Jason 3 that monitor sea levels, temperatures, and marine life. But back in the early years, NASA scientists were doing more than just watching the oceans. They were sending exploration equipment deep below the surface. The deep sea, Earth's final frontier. The deep ocean is one of the most mysterious places on Earth. It's estimated that over 80% of our oceans remain unexplored. The Mariana Trench, the deepest point on Earth, descends nearly 11 km, deeper than Mount Everest is tall. At those depths, sunlight never reaches, temperatures drop near freezing, and the pressure is crushing. NASA's scientists became fascinated by this alien-like environment because it shared similarities with outer space. Both are extreme, hostile, and largely unknown. For astronauts training for future space missions, NASA used deep underwater environments to simulate space-like conditions. The neutral buoyancy underwater helped astronauts practice moving in low gravity. That why NASA built facilities like the neutral buoyancy lab, a massive underwater training tank to prepare astronauts for the International Space Station. So, it's clear NASA's interest in the ocean wasn't random. But if the ocean was so important for science and training, why did they stop exploring it? The shift from the ocean to space? The official explanation is simple. NASA's mission changed. After the success of early underwater projects and satellite ocean studies, NASA redirected its focus to space exploration during the Cold War space race. In the 1960s, the United States and the Soviet Union were competing fiercely to reach the moon first. This intense political and scientific pressure pushed NASA to prioritize outer space over inner Earth. Funding was another major reason. Deep sea exploration is extremely expensive, perhaps even more than space missions in some cases, because of the technological challenges of surviving under high pressure and total darkness. Space missions, on the other hand, captured the public imagination and gained far more government support. Thus, NASA gradually handed over much of its ocean research to other organizations like no way, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, which now handles most marine exploration and monitoring. But even with this logical explanation, conspiracy theories started to grow. The conspiracy theories Many people believe that NASA didn't want to just stop ocean exploration. They found something that made them stop. This theory became popular after online users noticed that NASA had once collaborated with deep sea research programs and then suddenly shifted all focus to space. So what could they have discovered that was so shocking? Some say NASA s deep missions encountered strange unidentified creatures. massive and intelligent forms of life that humans had never seen before. Others believe that mysterious signals were detected from the ocean floor. Sounds or frequencies that didn't te match any known marine source. One of the most famous examples is the bloop, a powerful underwater sound detected in 1997 in the Pacific Ocean. It was so loud that it was picked up by sensors over 5,000 km apart. Scientists later said it was probably caused by ice movement, but others think it was something alive, something enormous. Then there's the theory that NASA found structures or technology hidden deep under the ocean, possibly remnants of an ancient civilization or even extraterrestrial origins. While none of these theories have solid proof, the fact that NASA remains silent about their early ocean missions only fuels more curiosity. What NASA actually says, NASA has addressed this mystery many times. The truth, according to the agency, is that they never truly stopped exploring the ocean. They simply changed how they explore it. Instead of sending submarines into the depths, NASA now uses satellite technology to study the ocean from space. Their Earth observing satellites collect data on temperature, sea level, and pollution, helping scientists understand climate change and global water patterns. NASA also collaborates with Noah and other marine research organizations on projects like PACE oceans melting Greenland OMG and SWAT which measure how oceans affect the Earth's climate system. So while they aren't sending robots to the bottom of the sea anymore, NASA's ocean work continues, just in a different way. But that still doesn't he stop people from asking what if there's more to the story. the real reason might be closer to science fiction. There's another theory that's less mysterious but more scientific. Some NASA researchers believe that studying the deep ocean actually helps prepare us for space exploration. For example, the extreme environments in the deep sea resemble those found on icy moons like Europa and Enceladus, places where NASA believes alien life might exist beneath thick layers of ice. By understanding how life survives in the crushing darkness of our oceans, NASA can predict what kind of life might exist on those distant worlds. So, in a way, NASA didn't t leave the ocean behind. They simply took what they learned from it and used it to explore the cosmos. The final truth. You Why did NASA stop exploring the ocean? The truth seems to be a mix of science, budget, and mystery. Officially NASA as mission changed. They passed ocean exploration duties to Noah and focused on space because that s where government funding and global interest were strongest. Unofficially, the ocean remains a place of secrets. A vast unexplored frontier that still hides more mysteries than the moon or Mars. Even today, over 80% of the ocean remains unmapped and unseen by human eyes. Who knows what lies beneath? New species, ancient civilizations, or something even more extraordinary? Perhaps NASA saw enough to realize that the greatest mysteries aren't just out there among the stars, but right here on our own planet, in the dark, silent world beneath the waves. In the end, maybe NASA didn't stop exploring the ocean because they lost interest. Maybe they stopped because they found exactly what they were looking for.

There are, as we know, huge gulfs of water just off the coast of California. Um the Baja California area is where the the US Nimitz incident happened in 2004. And I I guess I'm only speculating here, but if you were an alien civilization, you know, 71% of the planet's surface is water. title: New video shows mysterious flying sphere vanishing into ocean
transcript: a newly released video recorded by the u.s navy personnel two years ago shows an unknown sphere that followed a navy warship before disappearing into the ocean the video provides the latest glimpse into an ongoing pentagon investigation into ufos i-team reporter george knapp unveils details about the encounter earlier on today on our mystery wire website george ryan you know uh ufos are in the air if i'm here in the studio something's going on now this video was made public today by our colleague filmmaker jeremy corbell with analysis and background on mystery wire the unknown object was one of several that essentially surrounded the navy warship multiple times over three days four other navy ships had similar encounters over a period of several days in july 2019 at least five u.s navy warships based in san diego were swarmed by drone-like objects of unknown origin one of those ships the uss omaha was on maneuvers more than 100 miles directly west of san diego and beyond the channel islands one of the omaha's central missions is surveillance so it's packed with cutting edge technology and sensors but it appears the omaha was itself under surveillance this video was recorded inside the omaha's command center just before 11 pm on july 15. the image on the screen shows a six foot in diameter sphere that traveled alongside the omaha for about an hour it was pitch black outside so the image was obtained via a thermal sensor personnel in the command center made multiple recordings of what was on the screen at precisely 11 p.m the unidentified spear seemingly vanished into the ocean splashed when they say splash splash that doesn't mean there was a physical splash that means this object went into the water or they suspect it did filmmaker jeremy corbell obtained the video from sources he declines to identify in april corbell and mystery wire released a series of other startling images that are included in a briefing presentation prepared by the uap task force a unit created by congress last year to collect ufo-related images and information for a comprehensive report due next month one image from the uap task force is a freeze frame slide of the sphere seen beside the omaha the pentagon has confirmed that image was captured by navy personnel the task force considers it to be a genuine unknown other journalists have confirmed that multiple navy ships in the same vicinity were buzzed by unknown objects during the same time frame july 2019 corbell says as many as 14 of the spheres surrounded the omaha and were detected on sensors on and off during three perplexing days at sea the omaha's advanced sensors could not maintain a lock on the spheres as they approached the ship or when they departed they never found where these things came from or went to so they don't know where they're launching from or going from i mean maybe it's the water because that's what we're seeing one do mark bearing voices on the video confirmed the omaha launched an immediate search using all of its high-tech resources but when the spear vanished from the screen it also vanished from the omaha's radar and sonar no wreckage was found on the surface the uap task force briefing is a classified document but the images contained in that briefing are not classified that distinction was made so the task force could use images like this to brief other military and intelligence officials about the ufo mystery simply put the navy does not know what these things are they are still considered to be unknown

when a flash of light makes him pull over. It's not the police. It's this. STUART ECK: Oh my goodness. Awesome, at sunrise. HOST: A large white plume

title: Mysterious skies and seas: The latest in global UFO, USO sightings | Backscroll
transcript: Welcome to NewsNation's Back Scroll, where we look back at our major news coverage on the most important topics and personalities of our time. Um, there's nobody around there with a dive boat, with a dive flag, anything like that. I like to dive at night. Some people go lobster diving. It is still possible. This particular one um we haven't had time even to get out there to go check this out in the field. But um you start looking at you know the the number of sightings that people are having in these locations and um you know putting it all together it just doesn't make sense that so many people um you know are are unable to um I don't know it the UFO thing has been um you know always people focusing in the sky right and so it's it's kind of like now that we're shift shifting to USOS. Um, a lot of people are wondering if this is a new phenomena or not. And it really is not. You know, it's um the submerged objects have been uh since the beginning, you know, when we started reporting, remember, we had these pilots were seeing them in the water off of Catalina and air traffic controllers were telling us that um pilots were witnessing this glow sometimes the size of a city, you know, and so here have this Fort Lauder Lauderdale case. It's it's a lot smaller, but um you know, it's really hard to say uh whether we're looking at a natural phenomena. It's [clears throat] definitely not like phosphorus and algae. It's nothing like that. And it definitely doesn't explain how people are seeing the objects come up out of the water and zip out in these huge like flaps have been going on for decades. >> And then the ones that dive back into the water and don't even make a splash, which is also it just gives the, you know, makes the hair on my neck stand on end. Again, I just want to go back to the these pictures. Um, no. Okay, not divers, but is it poss Well, first of all, have we seen any, you know, plausible explanations surfacing like, well, this could be the military testing out new technology. This could be all sorts of stuff that we're not supposed to be seeing. Could be the enemy, although I doubt the enemy wants to be seen like this. But any plausible stuff that you've seen surface? >> Um, in this particular case, we really haven't had a chance, no pun intended, to dive into it yet. Um, but this is just an example, like I said, of of something that is is much bigger where I've had um numerous cases where people are telling us they've been out in the boat, they've been far away from shore, which again would be much more difficult to be just divers. And they've passed over areas about the size of a football field. And it's like somebody turns on a switch and the whole thing just lights up underneath them. Right. Uh come up out of the water sometimes in broad daylight, right? So something is traveling back and forth. Um you remember Rear Admiral Tim Galardet who's been on the show before. He testified in front of Congress about a Navy program that they're calling the Fast Movers, right? And the Fast Movers, they detect and track these objects that travel faster than any torpedo should or could, you know, um perhaps over a 100 miles an hour, right? And so you've got the fast ones, you have ones that kind of just linger there. Um huge kind of like uh the size of a city. These guys, I think, were really afraid because they didn't know, should we actually drive over it? What's going to happen if we do? Is it going to take us down, suck us in? >> Joining us now is Nick Pope. He is a former UK Ministry of Defense UFO investigator. Nick, what a pleasure. Welcome. >> Thank you. >> What do you make of this video? >> Well, it it's fascinating, but I don't think it's going to solve or resolve the UFO mystery one way or the other. I mean, we we simply haven't got enough data about it. And as the Pentagon themselves say, uh, you know, there there just isn't enough to say whether this is a sensor error or something else. And even if it's something else, I mean, we don't far it is from the camera. We don't know what speed it's doing. Some of these things literally turn out to be birds or insects, but it's probably not a balloon. >> Probably not a balloon. Do you think it could be a UFO, Nick? I mean, it it could be, but and and it is interesting, of course, that when the Pentagon's Arrow unit that has the lead now for UFOs uh released this latest batch of videos, pretty much all of them said resolved as balloon and this one wasn't. So, I guess any unresolved case is interesting. But I think in a way these videos are a bit of a distraction now. There are people, whistleblowers, coming forward to say there are legacy intelligence community programs which would confirm the existence of a non-human intelligence. That's what Congress needs to be looking at, not so much these videos, fascinating though they may be. >> It's you're absolutely right. We've shown a lot of those congressional hearings to our viewers and a lot of those uh people, those whistleblowers are saying all they want is transparency. You spent much of your career investigating UFOs for the UK government. Typically, when you see something that you can't explain, is it just, you know, checked out to be a balloon like these other cases that the Pentagon has analyzed and maybe not figured out? >> Yes. I mean, Aram's razor suggests that the simplest solution is usually the correct one. So, most times these things have prosaic explanations, but we never say never. And I mean, we're always looking, I suppose, for a smoking gun. You know, a video where you really would take this beyond speculation, but with the best will in the world, even though this is an interesting video, it's it's just basically a blob moving around and it's not going to tell you much without the data, which as I say, even the Pentagon say they really don't have. >> You and I talked about those congressional hearings a moment ago. So tell us more what how did you go about doing this and what did you find? >> Well we had the focus of having an instrumented field study for UAP. So, while we don't have some kind of final conclusive smoking gun evidence of something anomalous, what we've done is really help break down the stigma and the taboo by publishing our results in a major journal, Progress in Aerospace Sciences. >> So, you were able to identify most of the objects, but one you could not. What do you think that was? Can you describe what properties it had? >> That's right. So, we found one dark spot with white dots in it in one of our camera systems. Now, by itself, that could be anything. You know, it can be a camera artifact or glitch, but it seemed to have some correspondence at least to a small degree of statistical uh significance with an ionizing radiation event and with Doppler weather radar data hits using publicly available National Weather Service and Noah data. So again, not conclusive statistically, but the strange hint that there was something physical in the sky and that it wasn't just a a camera glitch um at the time. >> When was this? >> Like when did that happen? >> This was in um this was in July of 2021. So I went with a group called UAPX and we were part of a documentary film called A Tear in the Sky. And just, you know, very quickly, you you started to get interested in this because of the infamous 2004 tic tac video from the USS Nimmits. Um, that's what sort of sparked your interest in all this. Um, so that kind of solidified my interest, but I already had interest much earlier from childhood growing up watching Unsolved Mysteries and shows, you know, like that with Robert Stack and seeing all the famous cases like the Cash Landram and Rendlesome Forest incident and I just thought there's all these famous cases even before 2004 that I found the evidence compelling that there was a there there that there was something to to study and try to understand. >> And it's worth noting, we still don't know what that video, the tic-tac video, that's still a sort of unexplained mystery. The pilots who saw that in the sky are convinced it was something otherworldly. Uh Matthew Shadagas, fascinating. Uh come back and and tell us more when you have more, but it's fascinating and it's a great step forward for scientists to be studying this as well. Appreciate it. >> Thank you so much. No, it's moving too slow for a shooting star. >> Is that a UFO? >> That's a UFO. >> Oh, wow. >> Yeah. Just days ago, this was seen in the western Oklahoma City sky around 7:30 p.m. local time. Now, notably, SpaceX had launched a rocket earlier, but it would have been seen 180 degrees in the other direction.
